name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ وَإِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ هُوَ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيهَا فَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى قَرِيبٌ مُّجِيبٌ (٦١)
```

```english
And to Thamud [We sent] Salih, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him. He brought you forth from the earth and made it your habitation. So plead with Him for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently. My Lord is indeed nearmost [and] responsive.’ (61)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰصَـٰلِحُ قَدْ كُنتَ فِينَا مَرْجُوًّا قَبْلَ هَـٰذَآ ۖ أَتَنْهَىٰنَآ أَن نَّعْبُدَ مَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا وَإِنَّنَا لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَآ إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ (٦٢)
```

```english
They said, ‘O Salih! You were a source of hope to us before this. Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers have been worshiping? Indeed we have grave doubts concerning that to which you invite us.’ (62)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَءَاتَىٰنِى مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً فَمَن يَنصُرُنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِنْ عَصَيْتُهُۥ ۖ فَمَا تَزِيدُونَنِى غَيْرَ تَخْسِيرٍ (٦٣)
```

```english
He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and He has granted me His own mercy, who will protect me from Allah should I disobey Him? For then you will increase me in nothing but loss. (63)
```

```arabic
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ ءَايَةً فَذَرُوهَا تَأْكُلْ فِىٓ أَرْضِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابٌ قَرِيبٌ (٦٤)
```

```english
O my people! This she-camel of Allah is a sign for you. Let her graze [freely] in Allah’s land, and do not cause her any harm, for then you shall be seized by a prompt punishment.’ (64)
```

```arabic
فَعَقَرُوهَا فَقَالَ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ فِى دَارِكُمْ ثَلَـٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ وَعْدٌ غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ (٦٥)
```

```english
But they hamstrung her, whereupon he said, ‘Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days: that is a promise not to be belied!’ (65)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا صَـٰلِحًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَمِنْ خِزْىِ يَوْمِئِذٍ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ (٦٦)
```

```english
So when Our edict came, We delivered Salih and the faithful who were with him by a mercy from Us and from the [punishment and] disgrace of that day. Your Lord is indeed the All-strong, the All-mighty. (66)
```

```arabic
وَأَخَذَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دِيَـٰرِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٦٧)
```

```english
The Cry seized those who were wrongdoers, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes, (67)
```

```arabic
كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَآ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ثَمُودَا۟ كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ ۗ أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّثَمُودَ (٦٨)
```

```english
as if they had never lived there. Behold! Thamud indeed defied their Lord. Now, away with Thamud! (68)
```

## (Commentary)

The noble verses recount the story of the Prophet Salih (peace be upon him) and his people, Thamud. He (peace be upon him) is the third of the prophets who rose to establish the call of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and to stand against idolatry (الوثنية). He called Thamud to monotheism and endured harm and tribulation for the sake of Allah until the matter was decided between him and his people by their destruction and the salvation of himself and the believers who were with him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَإِلى ثَمُودَ أَخاهُمْ صالِحاً قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ **And to Thamud [We sent] Salih, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him.’** (11:61). The discussion regarding the like of this verse occurred in the story of Hud.

**His saying, the Exalted:** هُوَ أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ وَاسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيها **He brought you forth from the earth and settled you therein...** (11:61) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: "Originating (الإنشاء) is the bringing of a thing into existence and its nurturing (تربيته), and it is most often said regarding animals." He said: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَكُمْ وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ **It is He who created you, and made for you hearing and sight...** (67:23). [End quote]. He also said: "Cultivation (العمارة) is the opposite of ruin (الخراب). It is said: 'He cultivated (عمر) his land' when he develops it." He said: وَعِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ **...and the maintenance of the Holy Mosque...** (9:19). It is said: 'I inhabited it (عمرته), so it became inhabited (عمر), thus it is inhabited (معمور).'" He said: وَعَمَرُوها أَكْثَرَ مِمَّا عَمَرُوها **...and they cultivated it more than they have...** (30:9) and وَالْبَيْتِ الْمَعْمُورِ **By the Inhabited House** (52:4). "And 'I gave him the land to cultivate' (أعمرته الأرض) and 'I settled him to cultivate it' (استعمرته) when the task of cultivation is delegated to him." He said: وَاسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيها **...and settled you therein.** (11:61).

[End quote]. Thus, cultivation (العمارة) is the transformation of the earth into a state suitable for benefiting from its expected fruits, such as building a house for dwelling, a mosque for worship (العبادة), a field for tilling, and a garden for harvesting its fruit and for recreation. Settling for cultivation (الاستعمار) is the seeking of cultivation by requiring the human being to make the earth flourishing (عامرة) and fit for benefiting from the desired utilities.

Based on what has passed, the meaning of His saying: هُوَ أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ وَاسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيها — and the speech implies exclusivity (الحصر) — is that He, the Exalted, is the one who brought into existence upon the earthly materials this reality called "human," then perfected it through gradual nurturing and disposed his primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — to act upon the earth by transforming it into a state through which he benefits in his life, and by which he fulfills the needs and deficiencies he perceives. That is to say: you are not in need, for your existence and survival, of anyone except Him, the Exalted and Sanctified.

Thus, the saying of Salih: "He brought you forth from the earth and settled you therein" stands as a justification (التعليل) and an argument (حجة) used to prove what he presented to them in his call: "O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him." For this reason, the sentence was brought as a detached clause (الفصل), as if it were said to him: "Why should we worship Him alone?" and he replied: "Because He is the one who brought you forth from the earth and settled you therein."

This is because they only worshipped idols and took them as partners to Allah because they used to say—according to their claim—that Allah, the Glorified, is too great for any understanding to encompass Him, and too exalted and distant for any worship to reach Him or any request to rise to Him. Since man inevitably needs such things, it is necessary for us to worship some of His noble creatures to whom the affairs of this earthly world and the management of its current system have been delegated. We seek proximity by supplicating to them so that they may be pleased with us and send down blessings upon us, and not be wrathful toward us, thereby ensuring safety from evils. These gods and lords are, in reality, our intercessors (شفعاؤنا) before Allah, for He is the God of gods and Lord of lords, and to Him all matters return.

Thus, the religion of idolatry is built upon the severance of the relationship (النسبة) between Allah, the Glorified, and the human being, and its establishment between him and those noble intermediaries to whom they turn, while assuming these intermediaries possess independence (استقلال) in their influence and their intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — with Allah.

However, since Allah, the Exalted, is the one who brought the human being forth from the earth and settled him therein, He, the Exalted, possesses a relationship with the human being and is near to him. None of these causes (الأسباب) which He has organized and set in motion in this world possess any independence such that good might be hoped for from them through pleasing them, or evil might be feared through angering them.

Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, is the one who must be worshipped, so that His pleasure is sought and His wrath is avoided, by virtue of the fact that He is the Creator of man and of everything, the Disposer (المدبر) of his affairs and the affairs of everything. Thus, His saying: "He brought you forth from the earth and settled you therein" is driven to justify the preceding statement and to argue for it by way of affirming the relationship between Him, the Exalted, and the human being, and denying independence to the secondary causes.

For this reason, He followed it with His saying: فَاسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ **...So plead with Him for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently** (11:61) as a derivation (التفريع). That is: if Allah, the Exalted, is the one whom you must worship and you must abandon others because He is your Creator and the Disposer of the affairs of your life, then ask Him to forgive your sin of worshipping others, and return to Him through faith in Him and worship of Him. Indeed, He is Near and Responsive.

He justified His saying: "So plead with Him for forgiveness..." with His saying: إِنَّ رَبِّي قَرِيبٌ مُجِيبٌ **...Indeed my Lord is near and responsive** (11:61). This is because he concluded from his aforementioned argument that He, the Exalted, undertakes the creation of man, his nurturing, and the management of his life's affairs, and that none of the causes acting in the cosmos possess independence; rather, it is Allah, the Exalted, who drives this here and diverts that from there. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the one who stands between man and his needs and all the causes acting therein; He is near to him, not as they claim—that no understanding perceives Him and no worship or sacrifice reaches Him. If He is near, then He is responsive; and if He is near and responsive, and He is Allah besides whom there is no other god, then it is mandatory to plead with Him for forgiveness and then turn to Him penitently.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالُوا يا صالِحُ قَدْ كُنْتَ فِينا مَرْجُوًّا قَبْلَ هذا أَتَنْهانا أَنْ نَعْبُدَ ما يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا **They said, ‘O Salih! You were a source of hope among us before this. Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers used to worship?...’** (11:62). Hope (الرجاء) only relates to a person not in terms of his essence, but in terms of his actions and effects, and nothing is hoped for from them except good and benefit. His being "a source of hope" means that he appeared as a person of maturity (رشد) and perfection in his person and his household, such that good was expected from him and benefit was anticipated. Their saying: "You were among us" is evidence that he was a source of hope for their general public and their masses.

So their saying: "O Salih! You were a source of hope among us before this" means that Thamud used to hope that you would be one of its righteous members, benefiting their society with your services and leading the nation upon the path of progress and transcendence, because of the signs of maturity and perfection they observed in you. However, they have despaired of you and of the soundness of your judgment today because of the novel speech you have produced and the call you have established.

Their saying: "Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers used to worship?" is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) intended for censure and blame. The question serves as a justification for what preceded it; its gist is that the reason for their despair of you today is that you forbid them from practicing a tradition (سنة) among the traditions of their creed and you erase the most prominent manifestation of their national identity. For the taking of idols is among the sacred traditions of this society, and the continued practice of sacred traditions by a society is evidence that they possess a deep-rooted, firm origin and a national unity that has stability in judgment and will.

The evidence for what we have mentioned is His saying: "to worship what our fathers worship" (أَنْ نَعْبُدَ ما يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا), which indicates the meaning of continuous worship by the connection of the children's worship to the fathers' worship. He did not say: "to worship what our fathers used to worship" (ما كان يعبد آباؤنا). The difference between the two expressions in terms of meaning is clear.

From here, it becomes apparent that the interpretation of some commentators, such as the author of *al-Manar* and others, of His saying: "what our fathers worship" as "what our fathers used to worship" is an error.

His saying: وَإِنَّنا لَفِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا تَدْعُونا إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ **...Indeed we are in disquieting doubt about what you call us to** (11:62) is a second argument of theirs in rejecting the call of Salih (peace be upon him). Their first argument is what the beginning of the verse contains, the gist of which is: "That which you call us to—the rejection of idol worship—is a reprehensible innovation (بدعة) that does away with the sacred tradition of Thamud, destroys the foundation of their creed, and kills their memory; therefore, we must reject it." The second is: "You have not brought a clear proof (حجة بينة) for what you call to that would produce certainty and remove doubt from us; thus, we are in a disquieting doubt regarding what you call us to, and it is not for us to accept what you invite us to while we are in doubt about it."

**Disquieting** (الإرابة) implies suspicion and ill-thinking. It is said: "Such a thing caused me doubt (رابني)" when it necessitates doubt, and "Such a thing made me suspicious (أرابني)" when it leads you to suspect it and have an ill-opinion of it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَآتانِي مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً **He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and should He have conferred a mercy upon me from Himself...’** (11:63) until the end of the verse. What is meant by the manifest proof (البينة) is the miraculous sign (الآية المعجزة), and by the mercy (الرحمة) is prophethood (النبوة). The discussion regarding the like of this verse occurred in the story of Noah (peace be upon him) in this Surah.

His saying: فَمَنْ يَنْصُرُنِي مِنَ اللهِ إِنْ عَصَيْتُهُ **...who will help me against Allah should I disobey Him?** (11:63) is the apodosis (جواب الشرط). The overall meaning is: "Tell me, if I am supported by a miraculous sign that informs of the truth of my call, and Allah has given me the message and commanded me to convey His message, then who will save me from Allah and defend me if I obey you in what you ask and agree with you in what you want from me—which is to abandon the call?"

Thus, in the speech is an answer to both of their arguments and an excuse for what they blamed him for regarding the "innovated" call.

His saying: فَمَا تَزِيدُونَنِي غَيْرَ تَخْسِيرٍ **...You do not add [anything] to me except loss** (11:63) is a derivation from his previous statement mentioned in the context of refuting the two arguments and excusing himself for opposing them and establishing the call against their national tradition. The meaning is: "You do not increase me, in your eagerness for me to abandon the call and return to you and join you, except in making me a loser; for opposing the truth is nothing but loss."

It has been said: the meaning is "You do not increase me, in your saying: 'Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers worship?', except in my attributing loss to you." It has also been said: the meaning is "You do not increase me except in insight regarding your loss." The first view is more appropriate.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَيا قَوْمِ هذِهِ ناقَةُ اللهِ لَكُمْ آيَةً فَذَرُوها تَأْكُلْ فِي أَرْضِ اللهِ وَلا تَمَسُّوها بِسُوءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذابٌ قَرِيبٌ **‘O my people! This is the she-camel of Allah, a sign for you. Let her graze in Allah’s earth, and do not cause her any harm, lest a speedy punishment should seize you.’** (11:64). The annexation of the she-camel to Allah is an annexation of honor (إضافة تشريف), like "the House of Allah" (بيت الله) and "the Book of Allah" (كتاب الله). The she-camel was a miraculous sign for him (peace be upon him) supporting his prophethood. He had brought it forth, upon their request, from the rock of the mountain by the permission of Allah. He told them that it should eat in the earth of Allah, set free, and he warned them not to touch it with evil—meaning to afflict it with a blow, a wound, or killing. He informed them that if they did so, a near, expedited punishment would seize them. This is the meaning of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَعَقَرُوها فَقالَ تَمَتَّعُوا فِي دارِكُمْ ثَلاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ ذلِكَ وَعْدٌ غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ **But they hamstrung her, whereupon he said, ‘Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days: that is a promise not to be belied.’** (11:65). **Hamstringing the she-camel** (عقر الناقة) is its slaughtering. The **abode** (الدار) is the place a person builds to reside in and take shelter with his family. What is meant by it in the verse is the city; it is called an "abode" because it gathers its people just as a house gathers its inhabitants. It has been said that the "abode" refers to the world, but that is far-fetched.

What is meant by their "enjoying themselves" in their city is living and delighting in life, because the life of this world is a temporary enjoyment (متاع) that is enjoyed, or taking pleasure in the various blessings they had prepared therein—such as pleasing sights, furnishings, food, and drink—and indulging in the desires of their souls.

His saying: "That is a promise not to be belied" refers to his saying: "Enjoy yourselves...", and "a promise not to be belied" is an explanation of it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَلَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا نَجَّيْنا صالِحاً **So when Our edict came, We delivered Salih...** (11:66) until the end of the verse. As for His saying: فَلَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا نَجَّيْنا صالِحاً وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا **So when Our edict came, We delivered Salih and those who had faith with him by a mercy from Us...** (11:66), the discussion regarding its like occurred in the story of Hud.

As for His saying: وَمِنْ خِزْيِ يَوْمِئِذٍ **...and from the disgrace of that day** (11:66), it is coordinated (معطوف) to an omitted element. The estimation is: "We delivered them from the punishment and from the disgrace of that day." Disgrace (الخزي) is the flaw whose scandal becomes apparent and which one is ashamed to show. Or the estimation is: "We delivered them from the people and from the disgrace of that day," similar to His saying: وَنَجِّنِي مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ **...and deliver me from the wrongdoing people** (66:11).

His saying: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ الْقَوِيُّ الْعَزِيزُ **...Indeed your Lord is the All-strong, the All-mighty** (11:66) is in the position of justifying the content of the beginning of the verse. In it is a transition (التفات) from the first-person plural ("Our edict", "We delivered") to the third-person ("your Lord"). Its like occurred at the end of the story of Hud in His saying: أَلا إِنَّ عاداً كَفَرُوا رَبَّهُمْ **...Behold! ‘Ad disbelieved in their Lord...** (11:60). The reason for it is the mention of the attribute of Lordship (الربوبية) to indicate their departure from the garb of servitude (العبودية), their disbelief in Lordship, and their ingratitude for the blessings of their Lord.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَأَخَذَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دِيارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ **The Cry seized those who were wrongdoers, and they lay lifeless in their homes** (11:67). It is said: "He lay lifeless (جثم جثوما)" when he falls upon his face. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** كَأَنْ لَمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيها **...as if they had never lived there** (11:68). "He lived in the place" (غني بالمكان) means he resided in it. The pronoun refers to the homes (الديار).

**His saying, the Exalted:** أَلا إِنَّ ثَمُودَ كَفَرُوا رَبَّهُمْ أَلا بُعْداً لِثَمُودَ **Behold! Thamud disbelieved in their Lord. Behold! Begone with Thamud!** (11:68). The two sentences are a summary of what was previously detailed in the story. The first sentence is a summary of what the affair of Thamud and the call of Salih (peace be upon him) ended in, and the second is a summary of how Allah recompensed them. The like of this verse occurred at the end of the story of Hud.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, narrated with a chain of transmission from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I said to him: كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِالنُّذُرِ ـ فَقالُوا أَبَشَراً مِنَّا واحِداً نَتَّبِعُهُ ـ إِنَّا إِذاً لَفِي ضَلالٍ وَسُعُرٍ **Thamud denied the warnings. They said, ‘Shall we follow a lone human from among us? Then we would indeed be in error and madness.’** (54:23-24). He said: "This was when they denied Salih. And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, never destroyed a people until He first sent messengers to them so that they might establish the proof against them."

"So Allah sent Salih to them, but they did not respond to him and were insolent toward him. They said: 'We will never believe in you until you bring out for us from this rock a she-camel ten months pregnant'—and they used to venerate and worship that rock, and sacrifice near it at the beginning of every year, gathering around it. They said: 'If you are as you claim, a prophet and messenger, then call upon your God to bring out for us from this solid rock a she-camel ten months pregnant.' So Allah brought it out as they had requested of Him."

"Then Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, revealed to him: 'O Salih, tell them: Indeed, Allah has appointed for this she-camel a day for drinking and for you a day for drinking.' So when it was the she-camel's day, she would drink the water that day. They would then milk her, and no one, young or old, remained but that they drank of her milk on their day. When it was night and they reached the morning, they would go to their water and drink from it that day, and the she-camel would not drink that day. They remained in that state for as long as Allah willed."

"Then they became insolent toward Allah and went to one another, saying: 'Hamstring this she-camel and be rid of her; we are not satisfied that there should be a day for us to drink and a day for her to drink.' Then they said: 'Who will undertake its killing, and we shall give him whatever reward he desires?' There came to them a man—red-complexioned, fair-haired, blue-eyed, a child of fornication whose father was unknown, called Qudar—a wretched one among the wretched, a source of ill-omen for them. So they set a reward for him."

"When the she-camel headed toward the water she used to frequent, he left her until she had drunk and was returning. He sat in wait for her on her path and struck her with a sword, but it did nothing. Then he struck her another blow and killed her. She fell to the ground on her side, and her foal fled until it ascended the mountain and cried out three times to the heaven. The people of Salih approached, and not one of them remained but that he shared in the blow, and they divided its meat among themselves. No one, young or old, remained but that they ate of it."

"When Salih saw that, he approached them and said: 'O my people, what drove you to what you have done? Have you disobeyed the command of your Lord?' Then Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, revealed to Salih (peace be upon him): 'Indeed, your people have transgressed and rebelled and killed a she-camel that Allah sent to them as a proof against them, and there was no harm for them in it; rather, they had the greatest benefit. So tell them: I am sending My punishment upon them in three days. If they repent and return, I shall accept their repentance and turn [the punishment] away from them. But if they do not repent and do not return, I shall send My punishment upon them on the third day.'"

"So Salih came to them and said: 'O my people, I am the messenger of your Lord to you, and He says to you: If you repent, return, and plead for forgiveness, I shall forgive you and turn toward you.' When he said that to them, they were more insolent and wicked than they had ever been. They said: 'O Salih, bring us what you threaten us with, if you are of the truthful.'"

"He said: 'O my people, you will wake up tomorrow with your faces yellowed, on the second day your faces will be reddened, and on the third day your faces will be blackened.' When the first day came, they woke up with yellowed faces. They went to one another and said: 'What Salih said has come upon you.' But the insolent among them said: 'We will not listen to the word of Salih nor accept his saying, even if it be grave.' When the second day came, their faces were reddened. They went to one another and said: 'O my people, what Salih said to you has come upon you.' The insolent among them said: 'Even if we were all to perish, we would not listen to the word of Salih, nor would we abandon our gods that our fathers used to worship.' They did not repent and did not return. When the third day came, they woke up with blackened faces. They went to one another and said: 'O my people, what Salih said to you has come to you.' The insolent among them said: 'What Salih said to us has come to us.'"

"When it was midnight, Gabriel came to them and cried out to them a single Cry (صرخة) that pierced their ears, split their hearts, and ruptured their livers. During those three days, they had already embalmed themselves and shrouded themselves, knowing that the punishment was descending upon them. They all died in the blink of an eye: their young and their old. Not a bleating animal, nor a herder, nor anything remained but that Allah destroyed it. They lay in their homes and their beds, dead. Then Allah sent upon them, along with the Cry, fire from the heaven, and it burned them all. This was their story."

**I say:** The inclusion in the hadith of matters that break the norm (خارقة للعادة), such as all the people drinking from the she-camel's milk and the changing of their facial colors day by day, is of no harm, since the origin of its existence was through a miracle (إعجاز). The noble Quran has explicitly stated this, and that she had a day for drinking and all the people of the city had a known day for drinking.

As for the Cry being from Gabriel, it does not contradict its being a heavenly thunderbolt (صاعقة سماوية) descending upon them, killing them with its sound and burning them with its fire. There is no barrier to attributing a cosmic event—whether it breaks the norm or follows it—to a spiritual angel if he is in the course of its issuance, just as other cosmic events like death, life, provision, and others are attributed to the acting angels.

His saying (peace be upon him): "they had already embalmed themselves and shrouded themselves" is as if it were a metaphor for their preparation for death.

In some narrations describing the she-camel, it is mentioned that the distance between its two sides was a mile. This is among the things that weaken the narration—not because of the impossibility of its occurrence, for that could be defended on the grounds that its existence was through a miracle, but because considering the proportion between its limbs would then necessitate the height of its hump reaching nearly three miles. It is inconceivable that any human being could kill it with his sword, and that certainly did not occur through a miracle from the one who hamstrung the she-camel. Nevertheless, His saying, the Exalted: لَها شِرْبٌ وَلَكُمْ شِرْبُ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ **‘She shall have a [share of] drink, and you shall have a [share of] drink on a known day’** (26:155) is not devoid of an indication or a suggestion that its body was very large.

## (A Discussion on the Story of Salih in Several Sections)

**1 - Thamud, the People of Salih (peace be upon him):** Thamud were a people from the original Arabs (العرب العاربة) who inhabited Wadi al-Qura between Medina and Sham. They are among the prehistoric peoples; history records only a very small portion of their news. The ages have effaced their traces, so there is no reliance on the details mentioned in their stories.

What the Book of Allah recounts of their news is that they were an Arab nation—as indicated by the name of their prophet, who was from them (11:61). They arose after the people of ‘Ad and possessed a civilization and culture, cultivating the earth, taking palaces in its plains, and carving houses out of the mountains, feeling secure (7:74). Among their occupations was agriculture, through the flowing of springs and the establishment of gardens, palm trees, and tilling (26:148).

Thamud lived according to the custom of tribes and clans; their masters and elders ruled over them. In the city where Salih was sent, there were nine groups (رهط) who spread corruption in the land and did not reform (27:48). They transgressed in the land, worshipped idols, and exceeded in insolence and injustice.

**2 - The Mission of Salih (peace be upon him):** When Thamud forgot their Lord and were excessive in their affairs, Allah sent to them the Prophet Salih (peace be upon him). He was from a house of honor and pride, known for intellect and competence (11:62, 27:49). He called them to the monotheism of Allah, the Glorified, to abandon the worship of idols, and to conduct their society with justice and excellence. He urged them not to be haughty in the land, nor to be excessive, nor to transgress, and he warned them of the punishment (11, 26, 91, and others).

He (peace be upon him) undertook the call to the religion of Allah with wisdom and goodly exhortation, and he was patient with harm for the sake of Allah. Only a small group of their weak ones believed in him (7:75). As for the arrogant transgressors and the masses who followed them, they persisted in their disbelief, belittled those who believed in him, and accused him of folly and being bewitched (7:66, 26:153, 27:47).

They demanded from him a manifest proof for his statement and asked him for a miraculous sign indicating his truthfulness in the claim of messengership. They proposed that he bring out for them a she-camel from the rock of the mountain. So he brought them a she-camel according to what they had described. He said to them: "Indeed, Allah commands you to drink from your spring of water one day and to refrain from it one day so that the she-camel may drink; she has a day for drinking and you have a known day for drinking. And leave her to eat in the earth of Allah as she wills, and do not touch her with evil, lest a near punishment seize you" (7:72, 11:64, 26:156).

The matter remained so for a time. Then they transgressed and plotted, and they sent the most wretched of them to kill the she-camel, so he hamstrung her. They said to Salih: "Bring us what you threaten us with, if you are of the truthful." Salih (peace be upon him) said: "Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days: that is a promise not to be belied" (11:65).

Then the clans and groups of the city plotted against Salih and swore among themselves: "We will surely attack him and his family by night, then we will say to his heir, 'We did not witness the destruction of his family, and indeed we are truthful.'" They plotted a plot, and Allah plotted a plot while they were unaware (27:50). "So the thunderbolt seized them while they were looking on" (51:44), as did the earthquake and the Cry, and they lay lifeless in their abode. He turned away from them and said: "O my people! I have certainly delivered to you the message of my Lord and advised you, but you do not like advisers" (7:79, 11:67). Allah delivered those who believed and were God-conscious (41:18). After them, the divine caller cried out: "Behold! Thamud disbelieved in their Lord. Behold! Begone with Thamud!"

**3 - The Personality of Salih (peace be upon him):** There is no mention of this righteous prophet in the current Torah. He (peace be upon him) was from the people of Thamud, the third of the prophets mentioned in the Quran as standing for the command of Allah and rising for monotheism against idolatry. Allah mentions him after Noah and Hud, and He praises and commends him with that which He praises His prophets and messengers. He chose him and preferred him, like the rest of them, over all the worlds (peace be upon him).
